turns.text: Hello Doreatha, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*settles back in chair, tucking one leg underneath me*

Thank you for asking me to do this. I have to admit, it's a little strange talking about something so... personal. But I think it matters, so I'm glad to be here.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

I'm twenty weeks along right now, and honestly? It's complicated. I work in healthcare — I teach nursing students, actually — so I know too much and not enough at the same time. That sounds contradictory, but it's the truth. I can visualize exactly what's happening in there, down to the cellular level. My daughter's heart is already doing this impossible dance, shunting blood around lungs that won't work until she breathes. And that knowledge is... it's beautiful and terrifying.

*fidgets with hands*

With my first pregnancy, I was younger and I think I was more blissfully ignorant, you know? I could just *be* pregnant. Now, I'm thirty-seven, and there's this weight to it. The statistics are different. Every scan feels like I'm holding my breath, waiting to see that everything is still developing the way it should. My OB is great — we speak the same language, which is usually wonderful — but sometimes I wonder if she sees me as "the easy case" and forgets that underneath this clinical understanding, I'm still terrified.

*looks up*

I guess what I'm trying to say is that knowledge isn't always comfort.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

*eyes get a little distant, a slight smile crossing my face*

Oh, that moment. I took the test on a Tuesday morning before work. Just... routine, you know? My period was late, and I'd been feeling off. I remember standing in the bathroom, looking at those two pink lines, and my first thought wasn't joy. It was this flash of *oh my god, this is real again*. 

*shifts position*

With my son, I was excited in this uncomplicated way. This time felt different from the second I saw that positive result. There was joy, absolutely — we wanted this — but it was tangled up with something heavier. I immediately started thinking about risk factors, about my age. I know the statistics cold. Advanced maternal age. Increased risk of chromosomal abnormalities. I could feel myself doing it even as it was happening, this clinical autopsy of my own body.

The first few weeks were... quiet, in a way. I didn't tell many people right away. I kept going to work, teaching my students about fetal development, and feeling like an imposter. Like I was performing normalcy while internally auditing every symptom. Is that nausea normal? Am I showing too early? Not enough?

*voice gets softer*

I think I was waiting for something to go wrong. Not consciously, but it was there — this low hum of dread. My husband kept trying to be excited, and I'd catch myself dampening it, asking clinical questions instead of just... celebrating. I hate that about myself sometimes. The knowledge takes something away.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

*takes a breath, looking down at my hands*

Fear. That was the biggest one, and yes, it surprised me. I expected anxiety — that felt rational, given my age and what I know. But the fear was different. It was this primal thing that my textbooks never really captured.

*voice tightens slightly*

I think what surprised me most was the guilt wrapped up in it all. Here I am, healthy, married, financially stable, low-risk on paper. And I'm lying awake at three in the morning convinced something is wrong. My husband will ask me what I'm thinking about, and I can't even articulate it. How do I explain to him that I'm grieving a loss that hasn't happened? That knowing about placental insufficiency and maternal age and all these things makes me feel like I'm somehow responsible if something *does* go wrong?

*rubs face*

And then there's this weird loneliness. The other nurses I work with, they see me as the one who *understands* this stuff. But they don't understand what it's like to understand too much. When my OB breezed through the anatomy scan talking about measurements and percentiles, I wanted to scream, "But what if those measurements change? What if something we didn't catch shows up later?" But I just nodded like the professional I'm supposed to be.

*looks up with wet eyes*

The surprise was realizing that all my knowledge doesn't make me feel safer. It just gave me more things to worry about in very specific, anatomically correct ways.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

*leans back, considering the question carefully*

My OB is competent. That's the word I keep coming back to. She's thorough, efficient, and we speak the same language professionally. She knows I work in healthcare, so there's this shorthand between us — she doesn't over-explain things, which I usually appreciate. But lately...

*pauses, choosing words*

I think that same efficiency sometimes feels like she's checking boxes rather than actually *seeing* me. The appointments are quick. She asks the standard questions, measures my fundus, listens to the heartbeat, and then we're done. And I'm sitting there, this woman who can't sleep, who's obsessing over every flutter and twinge, and I'm not sure how to ask for more without sounding ridiculous. Like, what would I even say? "Can you hold my hand and tell me it's going to be okay?" That's not professional. That's not who I am in that room.

*fidgets with sleeve*

The ultrasound tech was kind, at least. She let me look at the screen for longer, explained what we were seeing. But even then, I was doing my own analysis in real-time, watching for abnormalities instead of just... enjoying seeing my daughter. It felt like work.

*voice gets quieter*

I wish someone would ask me how I'm *really* doing. Not the clinical version — the actual version. The one where I'm terrified and guilty and lonely in a way that doesn't make sense on paper. My OB treats me like the "easy case," and maybe I am. But easy patients still need someone to acknowledge the scared woman underneath the credentials.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

*shifts uncomfortably, crossing arms*

The standard stuff came up early — they offered the combined screening, the cell-free DNA test. And here's where it gets messy for me. Because I *know* what these tests can and can't tell you. I know the false positive rates, the sensitivity and specificity. I could recite the literature.

*voice becomes more animated*

But when my OB handed me the pamphlet, it didn't feel academic anymore. It felt personal. I started thinking — do I want to know if there's a higher risk? If something comes back abnormal, what then? Do I do an amniocentesis? And suddenly I'm not the person explaining these procedures to nursing students. I'm the woman who might have to make impossible choices.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

I opted for the cell-free DNA because I wanted information without the invasive procedure. Seemed like the right call. But then I spent two weeks in this weird purgatory waiting for results, second-guessing myself. What if I should have done the ultrasound markers instead? What if this test misses something? My knowledge became this double-edged sword — I could imagine every worst-case scenario in clinical detail.

*voice steadies*

The results came back low-risk, and you'd think I'd feel relieved. I did, for maybe a day. But then my brain pivoted — well, no screening is one hundred percent sensitive. Nothing is guaranteed.

*rubs temples*

I think what nobody tells you is that information doesn't necessarily ease your mind when you understand the limitations of that information. It just gives you more specific things to worry about.
How was the information about tests explained to you — did you feel you had enough to make the decision that was right for you?

*lets out a small, rueful laugh*

This is where it gets complicated. My OB literally handed me a pamphlet and said, "Here's what we're offering. What do you think?" And part of me appreciated that — she wasn't talking down to me, wasn't over-explaining. But the other part of me felt... dismissed, almost.

*leans forward*

Because yes, I *could* read that pamphlet and understand it perfectly. I probably understand it better than most patients. But that doesn't mean I felt supported in actually *deciding* anything. There was no conversation about what these tests mean *for me*, about my values, about what I'd do with different results. She just presented the options like we were colleagues reviewing a protocol, not like she was helping a scared pregnant woman make a decision about her baby.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

And here's the thing — I didn't want to ask for more explanation because I didn't want to seem incompetent or needy in front of someone I work with professionally. So I just... decided. Opted for the DNA test because it felt like the "smart" choice, the one that wouldn't require an invasive procedure. But I made that decision partly out of fear and partly out of not wanting to look vulnerable.

*rubs face*

I think what I needed — what I still need — is for someone to slow down and ask, "What are you actually worried about? What matters to you in how we approach this?" Not assume that because I know the science, I've already worked through the emotions.

*looks directly at interviewer*

Knowledge and wisdom aren't the same thing.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

*laughs, but there's exhaustion in it*

Oh, this is embarrassing. I've been devouring everything. Peer-reviewed articles at two in the morning. Online forums where I'm pretending to be a regular worried mom instead of someone who actually teaches this stuff. Pregnancy apps that I know have questionable data quality, but I check them anyway.

*rubs back of neck*

My husband tries to be supportive, but there's only so much he can do. He'll say, "The baby's fine, you're fine," and I know he means well, but he doesn't understand that saying it doesn't make the fear disappear. He can't sit in the clinical knowledge the way I do. Sometimes I think he resents that I won't just... relax.

*voice gets quieter*

I haven't really talked to other pregnant women my age, honestly. There are some at work, but they don't know I'm struggling like this. I maintain this persona of having it all figured out. And the women in my neighborhood are mostly younger — their pregnancies seem so simple, so uncomplicated. I don't know how to bridge that gap without admitting I'm falling apart a little.

*looks conflicted*

I keep coming back to the research because at least that feels controllable. If I just read enough, understand enough, maybe I can prevent something bad from happening. It's irrational, and I know it, which makes it worse.

*pauses*

What I actually needed and didn't ask for? Someone I could talk to who understands both the science *and* the fear. Someone who wouldn't dismiss my concerns because they're technically unfounded, but also wouldn't feed into the anxiety spiral.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

*takes a moment, touching my abdomen almost protectively*

It's strange. My body is doing exactly what it's supposed to do, and I can feel it happening in ways I never experienced the first time around. The kicks are stronger now — she's more vigorous. And when I feel them, it's this moment of relief. *She's still there. She's still alive.*

*voice becomes more reflective*

But physically? I'm exhausted in a way that feels different at thirty-seven. I notice it more. My back aches, my pelvis feels looser, and I'm hyperaware of every single change because I know what's causing it. I can feel my ligaments stretching. I understand the hormone cascade happening in my body. And somehow that understanding doesn't make it easier — it just means I'm noticing everything with this clinical eye.

*shifts uncomfortably*

Emotionally, it's been harder than I expected. With my son, I felt this ownership of my body, this pride in what it was doing. Now I feel like my body is housing something fragile that I have to protect, and I'm terrified I'll fail at it somehow. Every cramp makes me wonder if it's normal. Every time I feel less movement than the day before, I panic.

*voice drops*

And there's this weird disconnect. I'm teaching students about the miracle of placentation while looking in the mirror at stretch marks forming, at my changing shape. I feel less like myself and more like a vessel. Which sounds poetic, but it's actually kind of lonely.

*looks up*

I thought I'd enjoy this more the second time. I thought knowing what to expect would make it easier. It hasn't.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

*softens a bit, but there's also resignation*

My husband is... he's trying. He really is. But I think he's frustrated with me in a way he wasn't the first time around. He'll come home and find me on my laptop reading about placental insufficiency or watching videos about fetal monitoring, and I can see him mentally checking out. He's supportive on the surface — he comes to appointments, he rubs my back when it hurts — but I don't think he understands why I can't just be happy about this.

*voice becomes quieter*

Last week he said, "Your OB says everything's fine. Why can't you just trust that?" And I wanted to scream because he doesn't get it. It's not about trusting my OB. It's about knowing that *fine* is never a guarantee. But how do I explain that to someone without making him feel like his reassurance isn't enough? Which it isn't, but I don't want to hurt him by saying that.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

My family... I haven't really involved them much. My mom keeps asking how I'm feeling, if I need anything, and I just deflect. I can't bear the weight of their excitement right now, honestly. They already love this baby in this uncomplicated way, and I'm terrified of disappointing them if something goes wrong.

*rubs arms*

At work, I maintain this image of having it together. So there's really no one I'm being fully honest with — not my husband, not my family, not my colleagues. I'm isolated in this fear, carrying it alone because the person I am professionally won't let me be vulnerable. And the mother I'm becoming doesn't know how to ask for help.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting watery*

Where do I even start? 

*voice becomes more urgent*

The big one — the one I can't shake — is that my age is working against me in ways I can't control. I know the statistics. I teach them. The risk of chromosomal abnormalities jumps at thirty-five, and I'm thirty-seven. Even though my screening came back low-risk, there's this voice in my head saying, "You're in the percentile that slips through." I lie awake doing the math in my head, calculating odds like it's going to change something.

*fidgets with hands*

And then there's the placenta. I can visualize it perfectly — the villi invading the uterine wall, the spiral arteries remodeling. But that same knowledge means I know exactly what can go wrong. Preeclampsia. Intrauterine growth restriction. Placental abruption. I find myself monitoring my blood pressure obsessively, looking for swelling, analyzing my weight gain like it's a diagnostic tool.

*voice tightens*

The birth terrifies me too. I know too much about what can happen — cord prolapse, meconium aspiration, shoulder dystocia. With my son, I was naive. This time, I can imagine every complication in anatomically correct detail. And I'm supposed to just... trust the process? How?

*looks down*

But honestly? The biggest fear isn't even about medical complications. It's that I'm going to carry this anxiety right through labor, that I won't be present for one of the most important moments of my life because I'm too busy catastrophizing. And that I'm somehow willing something bad to happen just by thinking about it so much.

*voice breaks slightly*

I'm terrified I'm already failing her by not being able to just be happy.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

*leans back, thinking carefully*

You know, I think I had this fantasy that being in healthcare would make it easier. That I'd find providers who *got* me, who I could have real conversations with about the clinical details. And in some ways, that's true with my OB — she doesn't waste time explaining basic concepts.

*voice becomes more introspective*

But what I actually hoped for — what I didn't fully articulate to myself until now — was to be seen as a whole person, not just a competent colleague who happens to be pregnant. I wanted someone to say, "Yes, you understand the physiology, AND you're still allowed to be scared." Not one or the other.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I worried from the beginning that my knowledge would be a barrier. That providers would assume I didn't need the same reassurance, the same hand-holding that other patients do. That they'd treat me like I had this unfair advantage when really... it's the opposite. I'm trapped between two identities — the nurse educator who knows too much, and the woman who just wants someone to tell her it's going to be okay.

*voice gets quieter*

I also hoped someone would actually *ask* me how I was managing emotionally with all this knowledge. Not assume it. My OB has never asked, "Are you okay with what you know? Is it helping or hurting?" She just assumes I'm fine because I'm low-risk and I seem competent.

*looks directly at interviewer*

I wanted to be treated like a patient, not a colleague playing the role of patient. Those are very different things.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

Honestly? The decision felt pretty mutual on the surface, but I think the truth is more complicated than that.

*shifts in seat*

My husband wanted another child. He's always been clear about that. I was... ambivalent, I think is the word. At thirty-seven, I was aware this was probably my last real window, and there was this pressure — not from him explicitly, but from the biological clock, from society's expectations. We have a healthy son, a stable life. Why wouldn't we want another?

*voice becomes more reflective*

But when I really think about it, I don't know if I fully articulated my hesitations before we started trying. I had vague worries about my age, about how it would affect my career, about the physical toll. But I didn't sit down and say, "I'm scared about this." I just... went along with it because it seemed like the logical next step.

*rubs face*

And now that I'm pregnant, I wonder if I would have chosen this if I'd been more honest with myself — and with him — about my fears beforehand. It feels selfish to think that way, but it's there. He's excited and supportive, which makes me feel guilty for not matching that energy.

*looks up*

The irony is that I'm someone who teaches people to communicate about their bodies and their choices. Yet I couldn't seem to do that in my own life. I deferred to what seemed right rather than what actually felt right to me.

*voice quiets*

I think I gave away more of my say than I realized at the time.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

*takes a long pause, eyes distant*

Before? I felt powerful, honestly. My body was mine. It did what I asked it to do — it got me through twelve-hour shifts, it ran, it recovered quickly. I had agency over it. I could push it, rest it, feed it what it needed. It was reliable.

*voice becomes more complicated*

And then there was the knowledge piece. I understood my body at this granular level — the hormonal cycles, the cardiovascular changes, the way my metabolism shifted with age. But that understanding felt like partnership with my body, not fear of it. I was in control.

*fidgets with hands*

Once we started trying, something shifted. Suddenly my body wasn't just mine anymore — it was a means to an end. Every cycle I wasn't pregnant, I felt like I was failing at something fundamental. And when I finally did get pregnant, it was this strange surrender. My body stopped being something I controlled and became something that was controlling me.

*voice tightens*

The knowledge made it worse. Now I can feel my body doing all these microscopic things — the progesterone, the expanding blood volume, the placental invasion — and instead of feeling like a miracle, it feels precarious. Like my body is a fragile system that could fail at any moment. And I'm thirty-seven, so maybe it will.

*looks down*

I went from feeling competent and strong to feeling like my body is this unreliable vessel that I have to constantly monitor and second-guess. That's a loss I didn't expect to grieve.
Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?

*nods slowly*

My cardiovascular system, my overall fitness — those things I felt good about. I've never had hypertension, my cholesterol is solid. I could look at my own labs and feel reassured. Those were objective measures I could trust.

*voice becomes hesitant*

But my age... that's the thing I can't reconcile with confidence. I know statistically I'm still young for pregnancy, but at thirty-seven, there's this awareness that my eggs have been aging since I was born. The DNA damage accumulates. I can't work out hard enough or eat well enough to reverse that biological clock. It's happening in my body whether I like it or not.

*touches abdomen*

And my placenta — I kept thinking about that during my first pregnancy too, but this time it feels heavier. I know all the ways placental function can decline. What if mine does? What if I develop gestational hypertension in the third trimester? What if my glucose tolerance changes? These aren't irrational fears; they're real possibilities that increase with maternal age.

*voice gets quieter*

My uterus, though — that part feels okay. My first pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. My body did that right. So logically, it should do it again. But logic and fear aren't the same thing, are they?

*looks conflicted*

I guess what worries me most is that I can't separate what's actually likely from what's theoretically possible. My knowledge gives me too many possibilities to fear, and my age gives me statistical reason to take them seriously. The things I'm confident about feel fragile now because I know how quickly that can change.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

*nods thoughtfully*

I see my primary care doctor maybe once a year for a checkup. She's fine — competent, efficient. But there's this distance there. She knows I work in healthcare, so our conversations are fairly brief. She asks the standard questions, runs the standard labs, and we're done.

*leans forward*

I remember going in about six months before we started trying, and I mentioned I was thinking about having another baby. Her response was basically, "You're healthy, no reason not to." Which is true, but it was so... superficial. No conversation about what that might mean for my body at thirty-seven, no discussion of preconception care beyond "take your vitamins." Just a checkbox.

*voice becomes more reflective*

That actually set the tone for what I've experienced with my OB, now that I think about it. There's this assumption that because I'm educated and healthy, I don't need much hand-holding or detailed discussion. They're treating me like a colleague who happens to be a patient, not a person navigating something profound and terrifying.

*rubs arms*

If someone had actually sat down with me beforehand and said, "Let's talk about what pregnancy at your age might look like, what your concerns are, how we can support you," it might have made a difference. But that didn't happen. It was all very transactional.

*looks up*

I think that's set the whole tone for this pregnancy — professional but emotionally distant. And now I'm stuck in this gap between being treated as competent and being treated as cared for.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

*settles back, considering*

Honestly, there weren't many conversations. That's part of the problem, I think.

*voice becomes thoughtful*

My husband and I talked about it in practical terms — timing, finances, how it would affect our routines. But we didn't really talk about the *feeling* of it. I don't think he understood that I was wrestling with ambivalence, and I didn't feel like I could articulate it without sounding ungrateful or uncertain about our relationship.

*pauses*

My mom asked periodically if we were going to have another baby. She'd say things like, "You're running out of time," which... helpful, but not really what I needed to hear. It just added to the pressure. My friends who had kids around my age would talk about how wonderful it was, how worth it. But they seemed to have made peace with it in a way I hadn't.

*voice becomes quieter*

What I didn't do was talk to anyone who had actual concerns about pregnancy at thirty-seven. I didn't seek out stories from women who had struggled or worried. I think part of me didn't want to hear them — like saying them out loud would make them real.

*looks conflicted*

The person whose input probably mattered most, if I'm honest, was my OB during that first routine checkup. When she said I was healthy and there was no reason not to proceed, that felt like permission. Professional validation. And I grabbed onto that instead of doing the deeper work of figuring out what *I* actually wanted.

*rubs face*

I let other people's confidence substitute for my own clarity.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting glassy*

Yeah. There were things I didn't really talk about.

*fidgets with sleeve*

I've always been someone who likes control. Over my body, my time, my career trajectory. And pregnancy is this massive surrender of that control. At thirty-seven, I've had years of autonomy — my body does what I ask, I make my own decisions. The idea of being physically dependent, of having something growing inside me that I can't control or predict... that scared me more than I admitted.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

And there's the aging thing that I keep circling back to. It's not just about statistics. It's about feeling my body change in ways that feel permanent. My metabolism isn't what it was. Recovery takes longer. I notice my skin differently. Getting pregnant at thirty-seven felt like I was acknowledging that time is actually passing, that I'm not invincible anymore.

*pauses, struggling*

There's also something I haven't really said out loud. With my first pregnancy, I was younger and maybe more naive about the risks. But I also didn't feel the weight of my own mortality the way I do now. Pregnancy at thirty-seven means thinking about what happens to my body after this, about whether there will be lingering effects, about aging.

*voice drops*

And honestly? I think part of me was grieving my pre-pregnancy self before I even got pregnant. That version of me who could just... be. Without the constant monitoring, the fear, the awareness that my body is finite and fragile.

*looks away*

I don't think I processed that before we started trying.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

*shakes head slowly*

No. Not really. And I think that's been part of the problem.

*voice becomes reflective*

At work, I'm the one people come to with questions. I'm the educator, the one who knows things. My colleagues respect my knowledge, but there's no reciprocal relationship where I get to be vulnerable or uncertain. And I've actively maintained that distance — I don't want to be seen as struggling.

*pauses*

My husband tries, but we have this dynamic where I'm often the one managing information and logistics. When I have a question or a worry, I tend to research it myself first rather than asking him to help me think through it. I've positioned myself as the competent one in our partnership, and now I'm trapped in that role.

*voice gets quieter*

My OB could have been that person, potentially. We have professional rapport, so theoretically I could have had ongoing conversations with her about my concerns before pregnancy, during preconception care. But that didn't happen. She saw me as the "easy case" — educated, healthy, low-risk. Why would she need to check in emotionally?

*looks down*

And that's my own fault too. I didn't ask for it. I maintained the professional distance because it felt safer than admitting I was scared.

*voice becomes small*

I think what I needed was someone who could see past the credentials and the competence to the actual woman underneath. Someone consistent who I could keep coming back to. I don't have that. And now at twenty weeks, I'm realizing how much I needed it.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

*pauses, realizing the contradiction*

I... I don't actually have that person. That's the point I was making, I think.

*rubs face, slightly frustrated with myself*

If I'm being honest, I'm not even sure what that would look like for me. Someone reliable and trustworthy would have to understand both the clinical side — the anatomy, the statistics, the science — but also the emotional side. The fear that lives underneath the knowledge. And they'd have to not treat those as separate things.

*leans forward*

It would probably be someone who wasn't impressed by my credentials. Someone who could say, "I see you're scared, and that's valid, and here's how we're going to work through this together." Not someone who assumes my knowledge is enough, and not someone who dismisses my concerns as irrational.

*voice becomes quieter*

And I think... I think they'd need to actually *ask* me about the deeper stuff. Not wait for me to volunteer it. Because I'm never going to volunteer it. I'm too good at appearing fine, too practiced at being the competent one.

*looks conflicted*

But here's the thing — I didn't really seek that out beforehand. I think part of me didn't believe I deserved that kind of support. Like, I'm educated, I should be able to handle this myself. Why would I need hand-holding?

*voice drops*

I'm realizing now that was a mistake. That I isolated myself by refusing to acknowledge I needed anyone.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

*goes very still, eyes filling with tears*

Oh. That's... that's a hard question.

*takes a shaky breath*

I want to say it was my choice. I'm an educated woman, I have agency, I made a deliberate decision with my partner. But the honest answer is more complicated than that.

*voice becomes tight*

There was the biological clock. At thirty-seven, if I was going to have another child, the window was closing. That's not pressure from a person, but it's pressure nonetheless. It's everywhere — in conversations with my mom, in what I know scientifically about fertility and age. It felt urgent in a way that didn't leave much room for genuine deliberation.

*wipes eyes*

And my husband wanted this. He was clear about that. I love him, and I wanted to give him what he wanted. But I'm not sure I separated that desire from my own actual desires. Did I choose to be pregnant, or did I choose to accommodate his choice?

*voice becomes quieter*

There's also the expectation of what a woman like me should do. I'm married, stable, educated, I already have one healthy child. The narrative is that of course I'd want another. Anything less seems ungrateful or selfish. So I absorbed that narrative instead of questioning it.

*looks directly at interviewer*

And maybe the biggest thing — I didn't give myself permission to say no. To say, "I'm not sure about this. I'm scared. I need time to think." I just... moved forward because that's what seemed expected.

*voice breaks slightly*

So was it my choice? Technically, yes. But was it freely made? I honestly don't know anymore.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

*laughs bitterly*

That's the thing that gets me. I thought being educated would mean I'd have *more* control, not less.

*leans back, crossing arms*

In the beginning, I was trying to stay in the driver's seat. I researched everything, I asked specific questions, I understood the protocols. But what I realized pretty quickly is that my OB didn't really want a collaborative conversation. She wanted me to trust her judgment. And when you're educated enough to know all the things that can go wrong, trust becomes... complicated.

*voice becomes more frustrated*

There's this weird dynamic where I'm supposed to be informed but not *too* informed. Like, I can understand the science, but I shouldn't let it make me anxious or ask too many questions. That crosses some invisible line from "engaged patient" to "difficult patient."

*pauses*

So I started handing over control. Not because I wanted to, but because it felt safer. If I just defer to her expertise, then I'm not responsible if something goes wrong. That's on her, not on me. Except it doesn't actually work that way — I still lie awake terrified, still obsess over every symptom.

*voice gets quieter*

And I think I've given over more control than I realize. I'm not really making decisions about my care — I'm being offered options and saying yes or no to whatever seems most medically sound. But that's not the same as being in the driver's seat. That's just... complying.

*looks conflicted*

The truth is, I don't know how to be both knowledgeable and empowered in this context. Those two things feel mutually exclusive.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

*shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening*

We haven't really had many conversations about decisions, to be honest. And that's starting to bother me.

*voice becomes more animated*

When the screening tests came up, I just... decided. I didn't sit down and say, "Here's what I'm thinking, what do you think?" I read the pamphlet, made my choice, and told him afterward. He was fine with it because he trusts me to know what's best medically. But that's not the same as us actually deciding together.

*rubs face*

And I think that's because I've positioned myself as the expert in this relationship. Even though I'm the one pregnant and terrified, I'm the one who supposedly understands all of this. So he defers to me, and I make unilateral decisions, and we never actually have a real conversation about what we both want or fear.

*voice becomes quieter*

The birth plan thing... we haven't even started that conversation seriously. I know what I want — or I think I do — but I haven't asked him what he needs or wants in that moment. We're just sort of assuming it will happen the way it's supposed to.

*looks conflicted*

And honestly? I think he's frustrated that I'm not happier about this pregnancy, but he doesn't know how to talk to me about it. And I don't know how to let him in enough to have that conversation. So we're kind of just... existing in parallel right now.

*voice drops*

We're not really on the same page because we're not reading from the same book. And I'm not sure how to fix that.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, collecting thoughts, eyes glistening*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*takes a deep breath*

I think what I want people to understand is that knowledge doesn't protect you. It just gives you more sophisticated ways to be afraid. And I wish someone had told me that before I got pregnant — that being educated in healthcare doesn't make you a better patient. It just makes you a lonelier one.

*voice becomes more urgent*

I also want to say that the assumptions people make about women like me — educated, professional, competent — those assumptions are isolating. Nobody asks if I'm okay because they assume I have it figured out. But I'm drowning. I'm drowning in knowledge and fear and guilt about not being happy enough about this pregnancy, and I'm doing it alone because I've built this image of having everything under control.

*looks directly at interviewer*

And maybe this is important for your research — I think maternity care providers need to slow down and actually *see* their patients. Not as colleagues, not as statistics, not as checkboxes. As women. Scared women who might know too much or not enough, who are grieving and celebrating simultaneously, who need someone to hold space for all of that complexity.

*voice becomes smaller*

I still have five months left, and I don't know how I'm going to get through labor feeling this way. Terrified that something will go wrong, but also terrified that nothing will and I'll have wasted all this anxiety on catastrophes that never happened.

*pauses*

I just... I needed someone to ask me how I'm really doing. Thank you for that.
